turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh sure.

turns.name: JOHN J. CLEARY
turns.text: Okay. His relationship was unfor­ tunately with the woman, a Caucasian, who was using her White- Anglo status to transport the Latin-appearing individuals in the trunk of her vehicle. As the record indicates, she used that appearance, or she could escape detection by going through the San Clemente checkpoint. That status and that person was a critical witness in this case. Her credibility was an issue.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did you argue that to the judge?

turns.name: JOHN J. CLEARY
turns.text: I, in fact, at this time, I didn't know whether or not --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did you argue that to the judge?

turns.name: JOHN J. CLEARY
turns.text: I only said that -- I just --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What did you tell the judge was the reason that you wanted this charge?

turns.name: JOHN J. CLEARY
turns.text: I felt that the Court, under federal law, should permit a question as to racial prejudice when it's raised by counsel.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Pro se, pro se?

turns.name: JOHN J. CLEARY
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. And I cited all --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you have nothing beyond that?

turns.name: JOHN J. CLEARY
turns.text: Well Your Honor, the Court notes -- the Court didn't even permit me, the trial Court, to even finish the six questions that I had asked for and that -- my difficult for counsel to project all of the evidence in the and fully and honestly?

turns.name: JOHN J. CLEARY
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. In fact, I believe that that is --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do many people in the ordinary course of human experiences admit prejudices when they have them?

turns.name: JOHN J. CLEARY
turns.text: My experience, it has been yes. And in the case of -- up?

turns.name: JOHN J. CLEARY
turns.text: Yes, he did, he did have to stand. And I think that would only trigger the fact that his pre­ subject of racial bigotry, was even just presented in that fashion, and I think further, what's really critical about this case, is that the perfunctory nature of the question and further, after the voir dire, where counsel specifically Court's decision in Aldridge and the language in Ristaino v. Ross clearly set a different standard that would -- have, by my interpretation of it, set aside the Ninth Circuit prece­ dent. And I think that the question that Your Honor raises as to alienage as distinguished from race or nationality, this Court and Hr. Justice Marshall, in Espinoza v. FarahUManu- basis of alienage, that is to say, Mexicans, was not treading say as to race or nationality, that there was a clear cut

turns.name: JOHN J. CLEARY
turns.text: Race is a difficult term to define, but it usually includes not only physical characteristics, but certain ethnic delineations that have certain physical characteristics with it. And so, it has the physical char­ acteristics -- in my case, brown skin, a certain appearance, dark hair -- that then, the inquiry must be made. But I think maybe between certain European stock, as to whether such -- that might depend upon the cir­ cumstances . I think --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why would that be different? I mean, if you have neighborhoods in big cities where there are pre­ judices -- the German neighborhood right across the street from a neighborhood of -- say, a Polish neighborhood, why wouldn't you have the same right there?

turns.name: JOHN J. CLEARY
turns.text: Because I think, and again, I'm not trying to get into anthropology, I think the classifications would not define all of those -- I don't think you could call -- say, Germans, and French different races.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well but what difference does it make whether -- why is race significant to your argument at all? I think the -- I thought the touchstone would be potential prejudice ?

turns.name: JOHN J. CLEARY
turns.text: Well I think --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If you've got a group of people who may be the object of prejudice within a community, why does it the jurors whether any of them had any feelings about aliens, doesn't take the position that there would have been anything wrong with the judge asking the question, it's just that it wasn't necessary to do so? nothing wrong with asking the question in light of the ques­ tions that were asked, but in addition to that, the federal common law rule that Petitioner relies on does not at all suggest that the question should be asked whenever the De­

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well why?

turns.name: GEORGE W. JONES
turns.text: Because under the circumstances, it would be very likely that jurors would find his -- his status as Japanese --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Fact that he's Japanese.

turns.name: GEORGE W. JONES
turns.text: -- somewhat offensive, or at least be very sensitive.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You're really moving out, now. What about the Germans?

turns.name: GEORGE W. JONES
turns.text: Excuse me?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What about Germans and Italians?

turns.name: GEORGE W. JONES
turns.text: At that point, it seems to me that these two questions underscore the need to allow the trial judge and the district courts to look at the circumstances of the case and determine whether a specific question is necessary or not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well then, Mr. Jones, you are saying no more, I take it, than that it might be an abuse of dis­ cretion to decline to ask the question Justice Stevens suggested to you, not a constitutional per se rule?

turns.name: GEORGE W. JONES
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You weren't suggesting that there should be a constitutional rule on this?

turns.name: GEORGE W. JONES
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, of course, nobody's arguing

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: . That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's the way I am I right, Mr. Jones 7

turns.name: GEORGE W. JONES
turns.text: I think that's I am inclined to agree with your interpretation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would you think that would be equally applicable as to the need, now, the need from the defendant's point of view, if the defendant has a substantial criminal record but may want to take the stand? Would such a rule that we're talking about that's being advocated, indicate that the question would be required, would you as a juror criminal convictions?

turns.name: GEORGE W. JONES
turns.text: Well there are constitutional impli­ cations to that question that I am not prepared to deal with at present. But it seems to me that too is a case where there is no need for a per se rule, and a per se rule would be completely unnecessary, or inappropriate. The trial judge, looking at the case -- the defendant says well, I may or may not want to testify. The trial judge may have reser­ vations about asking questions but if the defendant can have his conviction reversed because the trial court refused to ask the question under those circumstances, it seems to us that the rule is charged with causing more problems than it is eliminating.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well what do you suggest, Mr. Jones, should be the standard by which, if it's not a per se rule, the trial judge should decide whether they will or won't ask the question that the defense counsel requests?

turns.name: GEORGE W. JONES
turns.text: The standard should be and I think, is, that only when the facts of the case or the nature of the charge suggest that there is some substantial likelihood that racial bias will intrude on the deliberations of the jury.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well counsel here had suggested to the judge, one of the reasons I'd like that question asked in this case is that it's likely to come out during the trial a in that circumstance, had he done so and the judge still refused to ask the question, Would you have thought that was the question?

turns.name: GEORGE W. JONES
turns.text: A closer question, Your Honor. But still a question of judgment, and one that the district court who has seen the answers of all the jurors to the questions asked before, is in a much better position to answer than I am.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well isn't it true, counsel, that in a case such as this involving illegal smuggling of aliens, people of entirely Caucasian background might have quite different feelings; some might be very sympathetic to someone who is smuggling illegal aliens because they could use them for labor on -- in agri-business, and that sort of thing, any people like that in this state. I mean the prejudice can border?

turns.name: GEORGE W. JONES
turns.text: Right. And the trial took place in San Diego. Rule 24 was initially considered. If the rule Is to be amended it should be amended by the rulemaking or rule CERTIFICATE North American Reporting hereby certifies that the attached pages represent an accurate transcript of electronic sound recording of the oral argument before the Supreme Court of the United States in the matter of: No. 79-6624 Humberto Rosales-Lopez v United States and that these pages constitute the original transcript of the proceedings for the records of the Court.
